title: Witch Week - Chapter 13 (Fragment 3)
text:
“Good morning,” Chrestomanci said politely.

“G—good morning,” said Mr. Wentworth. His hands were trembling and rustling the schedules. He swallowed, loudly. “I—I didn’t realize there were divisional inquisitors. New post, is it?”

“Oh, do you not have divisional ones?” Chrestomanci said. “What a shame. I thought it sounded so imposing.”

He nodded. Everybody was suddenly visible again. Nan, Charles, and Nirupam all tried to hide behind one another. Brian was revealed tugging crossly to get his arm loose from Chrestomanci, and Estelle was hanging on to Chrestomanci’s other hand again. She let go hurriedly and took her hard hat off. But it was quite certain that Mr. Wentworth did not notice any of these things. He backed against the window, staring from Chrestomanci to Brian, and he was more than harrowed now: he was terrified.

“What’s going on?” he said. “Brian, what have you lent yourself to?”

“Nothing,” Brian said irritably. “He isn’t an inquisitor. He’s an enchanter or something. It’s not my fault he’s here.”

“What does he want?” Mr. Wentworth said wildly. “I haven’t anything I can give him!”

“My dear sir,” said Chrestomanci, “please try and be calm. I only want your help.”

Mr. Wentworth pressed back against the window. “I don’t know what you mean!”

“Yes, you do,” Chrestomanci said pleasantly. “But let me explain. I am Chrestomanci. This is the title that goes with my post, and my job is controlling witchcraft. My world is somewhat more happily placed than yours, I believe, because witchcraft is not illegal there. In fact, this very morning I was chairing a meeting of the Walpurgis Committee, in the middle of making final arrangements for the Halloween celebrations, when I was rather suddenly summoned away by these pupils of yours—”

“Is that why you’re wearing those beautiful clothes?” Estelle asked admiringly.

Everyone winced a little, except for Chrestomanci, who seemed to think it was a perfectly reasonable question. “Well, no, to be quite honest,” he said. “I like to be well dressed, because I am always liable to be called elsewhere, the way you called me. But I have to confess that I have several times been fetched away in my dressing gown, in spite of all my care.” He looked at Mr. Wentworth again, obviously expecting him to have calmed down by now. “There are real problems with this particular call,” he said. “Your world is all wrong, in a number of ways. That’s why I would appreciate your help, sir.”

Unfortunately, Mr. Wentworth was by no means calm. “How dare you talk to me like this!” he said. “It’s pure blackmail! You’ll get no help from me!”

“Now that is unreasonable, sir,” Chrestomanci said. “These children are in acute trouble. You are in the same trouble yourself. Your whole world is in even greater trouble. Please, try if you can, to forget that you have been scared for years, both for yourself and Brian, and listen to the questions I am going to ask you.”

But Mr. Wentworth seemed unable to be reasonable. Nan looked at him sorrowfully. She had always thought he was such a firm person up till now. She felt quite disillusioned. So did Charles. He remembered Mr. Wentworth’s hand on his shoulder, pushing him back into detention. He had thought that hand had been shaking with anger, but he realized now that it had been terror.

“It’s a trick!” Mr. Wentworth said. “You’re trying to get a confession out of me. You’re using Brian. You are an inquisitor!”

Just as he said that, there was a little tap at the door, and Miss Hodge came brightly in. She had just given 6B an English lesson—the last one until next Tuesday, thank goodness! Naturally, she had noticed that four other people besides Brian were now missing. At first, she had assumed that they were all being questioned by the inquisitor. They were the obvious ones. But then someone in the staff room had remarked that the inquisitor still hadn’t come. Miss Hodge saw at once that this was the excuse she needed to go to Mr. Wentworth and start sympathizing with him about Brian. She came in as soon as she had knocked, to make quite sure that Mr. Wentworth did not get away again.

vocabulary.definition: Severe or intense in degree.
vocabulary.example: “These children are in acute trouble.

vocabulary.definition: Plans or preparations made for a future event.
vocabulary.example:
In fact, this very morning I was chairing a meeting of the Walpurgis Committee, in the middle of making final arrangements for the Halloween celebrations, when I was rather suddenly summoned away by these pupils of yours—”

“Is that why you’re wearing those beautiful clothes?

vocabulary.definition: The act of threatening to reveal damaging information to coerce someone.
vocabulary.example: “It’s pure blackmail!

vocabulary.definition: Leading or presiding over a meeting or organization.
vocabulary.example:
In fact, this very morning I was chairing a meeting of the Walpurgis Committee, in the middle of making final arrangements for the Halloween celebrations, when I was rather suddenly summoned away by these pupils of yours—”

“Is that why you’re wearing those beautiful clothes?

vocabulary.definition: A statement admitting that one has done something wrong.
vocabulary.example: “You’re trying to get a confession out of me.

vocabulary.definition: In an annoyed or angry manner.
vocabulary.example: Brian was revealed tugging crossly to get his arm loose from Chrestomanci, and Estelle was hanging on to Chrestomanci’s other hand again.

vocabulary.definition: The act of keeping someone in custody or confined.
vocabulary.example: Wentworth’s hand on his shoulder, pushing him back into detention.

vocabulary.definition: Disappointed after discovering something is not as good as expected.
vocabulary.example: She felt quite disillusioned.

vocabulary.definition: Relating to a division of an organization or police force.
vocabulary.example: “I—I didn’t realize there were divisional inquisitors.

vocabulary.definition: A person who uses magic or enchantments.
vocabulary.example: He’s an enchanter or something.

vocabulary.definition: Go for and then bring back something or someone.
vocabulary.example: But I have to confess that I have several times been fetched away in my dressing gown, in spite of all my care.

vocabulary.definition: Distressed or troubled, emotionally shaken.
vocabulary.example: He backed against the window, staring from Chrestomanci to Brian, and he was more than harrowed now: he was terrified.

vocabulary.definition: Done with great haste or urgency.
vocabulary.example: She let go hurriedly and took her hard hat off.

vocabulary.definition: Grand and impressive in appearance.
vocabulary.example: I thought it sounded so imposing.

vocabulary.definition: A person who questions someone closely or harshly, or historically, a judge in the Inquisition.
vocabulary.example: “I—I didn’t realize there were divisional inquisitors.

vocabulary.definition: In a way that shows impatience or annoyance.
vocabulary.example:
”

“Nothing,” Brian said irritably.

vocabulary.definition: In a way that is easily perceived or understood.
vocabulary.example: Wentworth again, obviously expecting him to have calmed down by now.

vocabulary.definition: Plans of activities or events with times.
vocabulary.example: His hands were trembling and rustling the schedules.

vocabulary.definition: To feel or express compassion or pity for someone else.
vocabulary.example: Wentworth and start sympathizing with him about Brian.

vocabulary.definition: Extremely frightened.
vocabulary.example: He backed against the window, staring from Chrestomanci to Brian, and he was more than harrowed now: he was terrified.

vocabulary.definition: Shaking involuntarily, typically as a result of anxiety or fear.
vocabulary.example: His hands were trembling and rustling the schedules.

vocabulary.definition: Able to be seen.
vocabulary.example: Everybody was suddenly visible again.

vocabulary.definition: The practice of magical skills, spells, and abilities.
vocabulary.example: This is the title that goes with my post, and my job is controlling witchcraft.
